content.text: pull claude.md into context and follow it

CLAUDE.md is already in context. I've reviewed the project instructions and will follow them. Key points I'm adhering to:

- Ask before coding, don't assume
- Get confirmation before large implementations
- Follow copyediting guidelines (smart quotes, conversational tone, atomic edits)
- Don't prompt with "what's next?"

What can I help you with?

Working on the about page hero. There's a component, src/_components/about-hero. First, thing. Just get markup in place for it. The hero will have five images. They are in images/about/2026/hero. For each there is an avif and jpg.

Can you:
- Add each one to the markup with the correct picture and src to fall back properly to the jpg if avif is not supported
- Add an alt to each with just "Portrait of Tyler Gaw", I'll change this later
- Set the width and height attr on each image to the width height of each image
- I'll want to use object position on these images and I'll want to clip them so, likely need to have a parent element
- If you see any structural css, go ahead and add it
Let me explore the component and images first.

content.text: Done. Each image has a wrapping `.about-hero-img` div for clipping (`overflow: hidden`) and `object-position` control. The `<picture>` elements fall back from avif to jpg. Structural CSS sets up a flex row with `object-fit: cover` on the images so they'll fill whatever size you clip them to.
Perfect
Good, ready for the next step whenever you are.
I *think* this all probably need to be fetchpriority high?
Makes sense — these are hero images, so they should load eagerly with high priority. Want me to add `fetchpriority="high"` and `loading="eager"` to all five `` tags?
Yeah

result.output: The file /Users/tylergaw/workspace/tylergaw.com/src/_components/about-hero.webc has been updated. All occurrences were successfully replaced.

content.text: Done — all five images now have `fetchpriority="high"` and `loading="eager"`.

Take a look at what I'm doing with the widths on each image now. Is this the best way to do this. I want the full space to always be filled and all the images be flexible width, but I want some of the images to be narrower/wider than others. Would flex-basis, or something else work better here?

Yeah, `flex-basis` with `flex-grow` would work better here. With `width`, the items won't naturally fill the remaining space — you'd need them to always add up to exactly 100%. With `flex`, you can set proportions and let flex handle the math.

You could use the `flex` shorthand to set grow + basis together. For example:

```css
.about-hero-img:nth-child(1) { flex: 1; }    /* narrower */
.about-hero-img:nth-child(2) { flex: 1; }    /* narrower */
.about-hero-img:nth-child(3) { flex: 1.5; }  /* wider */
.about-hero-img:nth-child(4) { flex: 1; }    /* narrower */
.about-hero-img:nth-child(5) { flex: 1.5; }  /* wider */
```

The `flex: 1` shorthand sets `flex-grow: 1; flex-shrink: 1; flex-basis: 0`, so all items start from 0 and grow proportionally. A `flex: 1.5` item will be 1.5x wider than a `flex: 1` item. You can also drop `gap: 0` since that's the default.

Want me to update it with proportions like these, or do you have specific ratios in mind?
Yeah, make that update

result.output: The file /Users/tylergaw/workspace/tylergaw.com/src/_components/about-hero.webc has been updated successfully.

content.text: Done. You can tweak the ratios as needed — just adjust the numbers relative to each other.
wait, somethign went wrong, can you repeat that change. Make sure you pull in the latest file

content.text: Done.
I want to set some of them to be less than 100% height, but `height: 90%`, etc on each nth-child is doing weird shit. What's the best way to do that?
Nooo, fixed height on the host is a no-go

Yeah, that's not great for a responsive layout.

How about using `align-self: flex-end` on the items you want shorter, and then scaling them down with `scale: 0.9` (or whatever) on the `img`? You're already using `scale` on a couple of them. Since `object-fit: cover` and `overflow: hidden` are in place, the clipping will handle it naturally — the image just shows less content.

Or, you could set a specific `aspect-ratio` on individual `.about-hero-img` divs to make some shorter than others, since the images will fill whatever box you give them via `object-fit: cover`.

Which direction feels right?

Right now, each image container stays the same percentage width, so whatever the flex is set to, that's how much of the image we see at all browser widths. What would it look like to allow that width (and viewable area of the image) do change, within a min/max, as the screen width changes?
[Request interrupted by user]
just breaking you out of spinning. try again, no code changes yet, just a response to my question
I implemented that mixed with some mqs to shape things up. worked great.
Nice, glad it worked out.

Right now in page-header.webc, the main nav links just have a border-bottom of 1px currentColor and no real hover/focus-visibile state. Help me do something fun. What if on hover, the bottom border "break" up and jitters similar to the chunky animation we have in scratch-lines and sorted like draw-strokes

result.output: /Users/tylergaw/workspace/tylergaw.com/src/_components/page-header.webc
/Users/tylergaw/workspace/tylergaw.com/src/_components/draw-strokes.webc

content.text:
Fun idea. Here's what I'm thinking:

Add a small SVG positioned under each nav link (like the draw-strokes pattern), and on hover/focus use JS to draw a broken, jittery line across the width. Redraw it on an interval (every ~400-500ms) for the chunky frame-by-frame feel, just like draw-strokes does. On mouse leave / focus out, clear it and the static `border-bottom` comes back.

The line would be a series of short segments with random gaps (the "breaks") and slight y-offset wobble, plus the SVG turbulence filter you're already using in both components for that hand-drawn look.

Approach:
1. Add an `<svg class="nav-strokes">` inside each nav link in the markup
2. Add a small JS block that hooks into mouseenter/mouseleave and focusin/focusout (same pattern as draw-strokes)
3. The draw function generates 2-3 broken line segments across the link width with random y-jitter
4. On hover, hide the `border-bottom` and show the SVG; on leave, reverse it

Sound right, or do you want to take it a different direction?
It'd be cooler if JS wasn't required for it, just CSS
Yeah, go for it. Let's see it

result.output: The file /Users/tylergaw/workspace/tylergaw.com/src/_components/page-header.webc has been updated successfully.

content.text: Done. On hover/focus-visible, the solid border goes transparent and a chunky jittery underline takes over — segments break and shift every 0.4s with a 1px vertical wobble. Take a look and let me know how it feels.
hrmmm, nah, that's not it. Roll that back.

result.output: The file /Users/tylergaw/workspace/tylergaw.com/src/_components/page-header.webc has been updated successfully.

content.text: Rolled back. What didn't feel right about it — too subtle, wrong vibe, or something else? That'll help me think of a better approach.
It was just a straight line with gaps in it. Just looked bad. Didn't look a single thing the other examples I mentioned

Yeah, fair. The scratch-lines and draw-strokes have that hand-drawn, organic quality — angled strokes, varying thickness, overlapping lines — and a flat segmented gradient doesn't capture any of that. CSS alone can't really generate those kinds of random, organic SVG paths.

I think to get that look, JS is the way to go — similar to the draw-strokes pattern that's already in place. It's the right tool for this. Want to revisit that approach?
Why just to JS? Why not just use a similar svg filter effect?

Right — you could apply an SVG filter (the same `feTurbulence` + `feDisplacementMap` combo from scratch-lines/draw-strokes) to the links on hover via CSS `filter: url(#id)`. That would warp the straight border into something organic and hand-drawn. No JS needed.

Define the filter once in the page-header markup, then just toggle it with CSS on hover/focus-visible. Want me to try that?
Yeah and this is just a svg line and there's only 3 links. We could have custom svg lines for each one and just fiddle with them by hand. This doesn't need to scale very much.
Now I've got the full picture. I'll add an SVG filter definition, put a custom SVG line under each link, and apply the filter on hover.

content.text: Done. Each link now has a hand-drawn SVG underline (two slightly offset wobbly paths) that appears on hover/focus-visible with the turbulence displacement filter applied. The static `border-bottom` hides when the SVG shows. You can tweak the paths, stroke widths, and filter values (`scale`, `baseFrequency`, `seed`) to get the exact feel you want.
Yeah, now we're talking. Now animated that effect with the similar choppiness that we do in scratch-lines and draw-strokes
